reason: Section 2.4 explicitly states relative variable importance in the random forest was determined using the Gini Index, and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, conditional importance, or held-out validation of the selected feature ranking is reported anywhere in the paper (the only performance metric given is 100% overall test accuracy, which validates the classifier, not the importance ranking). The ranking is not incidental: the paper explicitly interprets the magnitude ordering ("the computed t-statistics of metrics is definitively the most important... the second-most important factor... is the overlap score") to conclude that "the four empirical cluster groups are largely determined by the discriminatory ability of each metric," and this ranking is then used to select t-statistic and overlap score as the "top three most important predictive factors" characterizing clusters in Table 1, which underpins the paper's core recommendation of which metric clusters/scores to use. Because the predictor set mixes continuous distributional statistics (t-statistic, overlap score, min/median/max) with categorical variables (family, type, bound direction), this is precisely the setting where impurity/Gini importance is known to be biased toward high-cardinality continuous predictors, so the ranking claim is not robust to that known artifact and is not corroborated by any alternative method.
